title: CloudWatch Monitoring
content:
CloudWatch Monitoring CloudWatch monitors operational metrics for EC2, the ALB and Target Group, and RDS. Create SB-ALB-UnhealthyTargets after SNS and configure it to monitor UnHealthyHostCount for sportbooking-tg.
Resource Verified metrics Purpose EC2/ASG CPUUtilization, StatusCheckFailed Detects unhealthy or overloaded instances. ALB HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count, TargetResponseTime, HealthyHostCount Monitors backend errors and target health. RDS CPUUtilization, FreeStorageSpace, DatabaseConnections Monitors the database during operation. CloudWatch Alarm Procedure Use a Target Group metric, require the threshold for two consecutive periods, and send the alarm notification through SNS.
Procedure:
Start creating the CloudWatch alarm
Open CloudWatch > Alarms, then choose Create alarm.
Figure 1: Start creating the CloudWatch alarm
An alarm provides early detection when EC2, the ALB, or RDS fails or a metric crosses its threshold. Select a metric
Choose Select metric, then select the ApplicationELB namespace.
Figure 2: Select a metric
This alarm focuses on targets that fail health checks behind the ALB. Open the ApplicationELB namespace
Open the Application Load Balancer metric category and select the metric type associated with a Target Group or Load Balancer.
Figure 3: Select the ApplicationELB namespace
ALB metrics indicate whether backends are healthy and whether users encounter 5xx errors. Select the specific metric
Select UnHealthyHostCount for sportbooking-alb and sportbooking-tg, then choose Select metric.
Figure 4: Select the specific metric
This metric increases when one or more EC2 application instances behind the ALB fail their health checks. Set the alarm condition
Select Maximum as the statistic and set the period to 1 minute.
Figure 5: Set the alarm condition
This statistic and period capture the highest unhealthy-target count in each one-minute interval. Configure the threshold and datapoints
Set the condition to UnHealthyHostCount >= 1 and Datapoints to alarm to 2 out of 2. Keep Treat missing data as missing, then choose Next.
Figure 6: Configure the threshold and datapoints
Requiring two datapoints reduces false alerts caused by one brief anomalous data point. Select the notification action
Select the alarm state that sends a notification, then select the SNS topic created earlier.
Figure 7: Select the notification action
An alarm requires a notification channel to be operationally useful. SNS sends email when the system has a problem. Skip unused additional actions
Do not configure EC2, Auto Scaling, or Systems Manager actions for this alarm. Choose Next.
Figure 8: Skip unused additional actions
This alarm sends a notification through SNS and does not modify resources automatically. Name the alarm
Enter SB-ALB-UnhealthyTargets as the alarm name. Leave the description and tags empty for this creation, then choose Next.
Figure 9: Name the alarm
The alarm name should identify the resource and condition so the issue is immediately clear in an email. Review and create the alarm
Review the metric, condition, action, and name, then choose Create alarm.
Figure 10: Review and create the alarm
Validate the metric, condition, SNS action, and alarm name before creation. Verify the alarm in the list Return to Alarms and confirm that SB-ALB-UnhealthyTargets appears with actions enabled and an initial Insufficient data state.
Figure 11: Verify the alarm in the list
The OK, Alarm, and Insufficient data states show whether CloudWatch has received metrics and evaluated the condition.

title: Data Cleanup
content:
9. Delete the RDS MySQL Database Procedure:
Start deleting the DB instance
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) open RDS > Databases and select sportbooking-mysql; (2) open Actions; and (3) select Delete.
Figure 1: Select the RDS MySQL database to delete
The EC2 instances have terminated, so no application connection continues to use the database. Configure data retention and confirm
Select Create final snapshot and enter sportbooking-mysql-snapshot as the snapshot name. Select Retain automated backups to preserve recovery capability. Finally, (1) enter delete me; and (2) select Delete.
Figure 2: Configure the snapshot and confirm RDS deletion
The `sportbooking-mysql-snapshot` final snapshot and automated backup were retained according to the post-deletion storage plan. Verify that the DB instance has been deleted
Wait for the deletion process to finish, then confirm the Successfully deleted DB instance sportbooking-mysql message.
Figure 3: Verify that the RDS MySQL database has been deleted
The DB instance no longer incurs compute charges, but the selected snapshot and retained backup remain storage resources that require management. The final snapshot and retained automated backup can continue to incur storage charges after the DB instance is deleted. Monitor both resources according to the agreed retention period.
10. Empty and Delete the S3 Bucket The artifact bucket was emptied before deletion. The upload bucket was retained during this cleanup to preserve images uploaded by users.
Procedure:
Select the bucket and start emptying it
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) select the sportbooking-artifacts-3stars-us-east-1 bucket; and (2) select Empty.
Figure 4: Select the S3 artifact bucket to empty
S3 does not allow a bucket to be deleted while it contains objects, object versions, or delete markers. Confirm that the bucket will be emptied
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) enter permanently delete; and (2) select Empty.
Figure 5: Confirm that the S3 bucket will be emptied
Emptying the bucket permanently removes its data unless another backup is available. Verify that the bucket is empty
Confirm the Successfully emptied bucket status. Review the counts of successfully deleted and failed objects.
Figure 6: Verify that the S3 bucket has been emptied
In the recorded result, two objects totaling 82.5 MB were deleted and no object failed. Select the bucket for deletion
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) return to the bucket list and select the artifact bucket that was just emptied; and (2) select Delete.
Figure 7: Select the S3 artifact bucket for deletion
Delete only the project bucket. A `cf-templates-...` bucket may be used by CloudFormation or another environment and must not be deleted without verification. Confirm the bucket name
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) enter the exact bucket name; and (2) select Delete bucket.
Figure 8: Confirm S3 artifact bucket deletion
S3 bucket names are globally unique. After deletion, the same name is not guaranteed to remain available for reuse. Verify that the bucket has been deleted
Confirm the Successfully deleted bucket message, then verify that the artifact bucket no longer appears in the list.
Figure 9: Verify that the S3 artifact bucket has been deleted
The `sportbooking-uploads-3stars-us-east-1` bucket remains in the list after the artifact bucket is deleted. This bucket is intentionally retained and is not an overlooked resource. 11. Schedule Secret Deletion Procedure:
Select the secret for deletion
Open the sportbooking/prod/app-env secret. Then (1) open Actions; and (2) select Delete secret.
Figure 10: Select the secret to delete
The EC2 instances and IAM role have been deleted, so no workload within the project scope continues to read the secret. Set the recovery window and schedule deletion
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) set Waiting period to 7 days or the period required by the retention policy; and (2) select Schedule deletion.
Figure 11: Schedule deletion of the Secrets Manager secret
Secrets Manager does not delete the secret immediately. It places the secret in a recovery window during which the secret is disabled and can be restored if the deletion was accidental. Do not include secret values in screenshots or report content. If redeployment is required during the recovery window, restore the secret before updating it instead of creating a duplicate name.
12. Delete the ACM Certificate Delete the ACM certificate only when the In use column displays No. If an ALB or CloudFront distribution still uses the certificate, remove that association first.
Procedure:
Select the certificate
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) select the certificate for sport.younglilliu.id.vn and verify In use = No; and (2) open More actions and select Delete.
Figure 12: Select the ACM certificate to delete
The ALB has been deleted, so its HTTPS listener no longer holds the certificate. No CloudFront distribution was created, so the certificate has no additional CloudFront dependency. Confirm certificate deletion
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) enter delete; and (2) select Delete.
Figure 13: Confirm ACM certificate deletion
After deletion, the certificate can no longer secure HTTPS connections and cannot be restored. Verify that the certificate has been deleted
Confirm the Successfully deleted certificate message, then verify that the project certificate no longer appears in the list.
Figure 14: Verify that the ACM certificate has been deleted
The certificate-validation CNAME record can now be removed from Route 53 if no other certificate uses it.

title: Network and Security
content:
Deployment Scope Build the SportBooking network layer with a VPC, subnets, an Internet Gateway, NAT Gateways, route tables, and Security Groups. Network resources are created before storage, database, and compute resources so later services can use an existing VPC, subnets, and security rules.
Contents Create the VPC, Subnets, and Route Tables Configure Security Groups Network Design Component Configuration VPC sportbooking-vpc, CIDR 10.20.0.0/16. Availability Zones Two AZs distribute the ALB, EC2, and RDS resources. Public subnets Two public subnets for the ALB and NAT Gateways. Private application subnets Two private subnets for EC2 application instances in the Auto Scaling Group. Private database subnets Two private subnets for the RDS DB subnet group. NAT Gateways Two NAT Gateways, one in each Availability Zone. Recorded result: the ALB is the only public component that receives Internet requests; neither the EC2 application nor RDS is public.

title: Secrets Manager
content:
Deployment Scope Create the sportbooking/prod/app-env secret after RDS reaches the Available state. Store the RDS endpoint and credentials together with the application’s environment variables; during the bootstrap process in section 5.5, EC2 reads this secret to create /opt/sportbooking/app.env.
Configuration Variables Variable Value used Purpose DB_URL jdbc:mysql://sportbooking-mysql.cudgu8qk8z19.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com:3306/sport_booking?useSSL=true&serverTimezone=Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh Connects to Amazon RDS for MySQL. DB_USERNAME sportbooking_ad Database account. DB_PASSWORD Value concealed in the image Database password. SPRING_JPA_HIBERNATE_DDL_AUTO update Allows schema updates during the test deployment. APP_BASE_URL https://sport.younglilliu.id.vn Primary application domain. APP_UPLOADS_BASE_URL https://sport.younglilliu.id.vn/uploads Base URL for uploaded images. SERVER_FORWARD_HEADERS_STRATEGY framework Allows Spring to interpret X-Forwarded-* headers correctly behind ALB HTTPS. APP_STORAGE_TYPE s3 Enables S3 image storage. APP_STORAGE_S3_BUCKET sportbooking-uploads-3stars-us-east-1 Upload image bucket. APP_STORAGE_S3_KEY_PREFIX uploads Image object prefix. AWS_REGION, AWS_DEFAULT_REGION, AWS_S3_REGION us-east-1 Keeps the AWS SDK and CLI Region consistent. Do not expose DB_PASSWORD, OAuth client secrets, tokens, or secret values in report images. The evidence only needs to show that the required configuration keys and correct secret name exist.
Verify Secret Read Access After EC2 Deployment After the EC2 application is created in section 5.5, use Session Manager to verify secret read access:
sudo -i aws secretsmanager get-secret-value \ --secret-id sportbooking/prod/app-env \ --region us-east-1 \ --query SecretString \ --output text | jq -r 'to_entries[] | "\(.key)=\(.value)"' > /tmp/app.env grep -n -E "APP_BASE_URL|APP_STORAGE_TYPE|APP_STORAGE_S3_BUCKET|AWS_REGION" /tmp/app.env Display only non-sensitive keys with grep in the evidence. Do not print the complete environment file or any secret values to the screen.
Procedure Store the configuration as key/value pairs, name the secret by environment, and confirm that it exists before granting EC2 access.
Procedure:
Start creating the secret
Open Secrets Manager > Secrets, then choose Store a new secret.
Figure 1: Start creating the secret
The secret separates sensitive configuration from the source code and JAR, especially the database password, OAuth secret, and storage configuration. Select the secret type and enter key/value pairs
Select Other type of secret, then enter key/value pairs such as DB_URL, DB_USERNAME, APP_BASE_URL, and APP_STORAGE_TYPE.
Figure 2: Select the secret type and enter key/value pairs
The Spring Boot application uses multiple environment variables, so key/value storage can be converted directly into `/opt/sportbooking/app.env` when EC2 starts. Complete the environment variables
Add the remaining keys for the S3 bucket, upload prefix, AWS Region, and OAuth configuration, then choose Next.
Figure 3: Complete the environment variables
A missing variable such as `AWS_S3_REGION` or `APP_STORAGE_S3_BUCKET` can break image uploads even when EC2 and RDS are running. Name the secret
Enter sportbooking/prod/app-env as the secret name, add a description identifying the SportBooking deployment environment, and choose Next.
Figure 4: Name the secret
Keep the secret name stable so it matches references in the IAM policy and User Data. Configure rotation
Automatic rotation is not enabled within this deployment scope. Choose Next.
Figure 5: Configure rotation
Rotation can be enabled in a later operational phase when the application and database account support synchronized password changes. Review and store the secret
Review all non-sensitive keys and values. Conceal passwords and tokens in report images, then choose Store.
Figure 6: Review and store the secret
Verify key names and the Region before saving, and conceal every sensitive value in the report. Confirm that the secret exists
Return to the Secrets list and confirm that sportbooking/prod/app-env appears.
Figure 7: Confirm that the secret exists
This evidence supports the next IAM Role step: the EC2 Role requires `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` permission on this secret.

title: Target Group and ALB
content:
Target Group and Application Load Balancer The Target Group health-checks the EC2 application on port 8080. The Application Load Balancer is the public entry point that accepts HTTP/HTTPS requests and forwards them to the Target Group.
Component Configuration Target type Instances Target Group protocol/port HTTP: 8080 Health check path /actuator/health ALB scheme Internet-facing ALB subnets Two public subnets in two AZs ALB Security Group Allows inbound 80/443 from the Internet Initial listener HTTP:80 forwards to sportbooking-tg Recorded result: the Target Group uses the correct health check path, the ALB is active, and its DNS name can be tested before the domain is configured.
Target Group and ALB Procedure Create and validate the Target Group health check before the public ALB sends traffic to the application, then test the website through the ALB DNS name.
Procedure:
Start creating the Target Group
Open EC2 > Target Groups, then choose Create target group.
Figure 1: Start creating the Target Group
The Target Group receives ALB requests for the EC2 application and performs health checks. Select the target type and protocol
Select Instances as the target type, enter the Target Group name, and select HTTP on port 8080.
Figure 2: Select the target type and protocol
The Spring Boot application runs on EC2 port 8080. The ALB accepts public traffic on 80/443 and forwards it internally to 8080. Select the VPC and protocol version
Select the SportBooking VPC and retain HTTP1 unless the application requires another protocol version.
Figure 3: Select the VPC and protocol version
The Target Group must be in the same VPC as the EC2 application before instances can be registered. Configure the health check
Enter /actuator/health as the health check path and retain HTTP as the protocol.
Figure 4: Configure the health check
The health check allows the ALB to send traffic only to healthy application instances. `/actuator/health` reports Spring Boot status more accurately than `/`. Continue to target registration
Review the target selection and attributes, then choose Next.
Figure 5: Complete the Target Group configuration and continue to target registration
After protocol and health check settings are complete, register the seed EC2 instance to test the ALB before creating the ASG. Select the seed EC2 instance as the initial target
Select sportbooking-seed, enter port 8080, and choose Include as pending below.
Figure 6: Select sportbooking-seed as the target on port 8080
Register the seed instance to test the Target Group and ALB first; the Auto Scaling Group subsequently manages registration for the instances it creates. Review the target
Verify sportbooking-seed, port 8080, and the Running state in the pending-target list, then choose Create target group.
Figure 7: Review the target
An incorrect target port is a common cause of failed health checks. Confirm Target Group creation
Review the /actuator/health health check and sportbooking-seed target, then choose Create target group.
Figure 8: Confirm Target Group creation
This final step makes the Target Group available for the ALB and ASG. Confirm the pending target registration
When the AWS Console displays the pending-target confirmation dialog, choose Continue.
Figure 9: Confirm the pending target registration
AWS warns that the target is awaiting a health check. The state changes to Healthy after the application runs successfully for several minutes. Verify the Target Group
Open the new Target Group and inspect its Targets and health check settings.
Figure 10: Verify the Target Group
A Healthy target is an important prerequisite before the domain points to the ALB. Start creating the Load Balancer Open EC2 > Load Balancers, then choose Create load balancer.
Figure 11: Start creating the Load Balancer
The Load Balancer is the website's public entry point, replacing direct access to private EC2 instances. Select Application Load Balancer Select Application Load Balancer, then choose Create.
Figure 12: Select Application Load Balancer
An ALB supports HTTP/HTTPS web traffic, listeners, rules, Target Groups, and TLS termination. Enter the basic ALB configuration Enter sportbooking-alb as the name, select the Internet-facing scheme, and use the IPv4 address type.
Figure 13: Enter the basic ALB configuration
The Internet-facing ALB receives user traffic while the EC2 application remains private. Select the VPC and public subnets Select the SportBooking VPC and two public subnets in two AZs.
Figure 14: Select the VPC and public subnets
The ALB requires public subnets to receive Internet requests and multiple AZs for improved availability. Select the Security Group and listener Select the ALB Security Group that allows ports 80 and 443. Create the initial HTTP:80 listener and configure it to forward to sportbooking-tg.
Figure 15: Select the Security Group and listener
The ALB Security Group is the only public access point. The listener forwards requests to the application through the Target Group. Review and create the ALB Review the VPC, subnets, Security Group, listener, and Target Group, then choose Create load balancer.
Figure 16: Review and create the ALB
Validate the VPC, two public subnets, Security Group, listener, and Target Group before creating the ALB. Confirm that the ALB was created Open the new ALB and record sportbooking-alb-1198360694.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com as its DNS name.
Figure 17: Confirm that the ALB was created
Test with the ALB DNS name before configuring the Route 53 Alias. Verify listeners and security Open the listener and security tabs and confirm that the HTTP listener forwards to the correct Target Group.
Figure 18: Verify listeners and security
An incorrect listener action can make the ALB reachable from a browser while preventing requests from reaching the application. Test the website through the ALB Open the ALB DNS name or the configured domain and confirm that the SportBooking home page loads.
Figure 19: Test the website through the ALB
This is end-to-end evidence of the path: user -> ALB -> Target Group -> EC2 application -> RDS/S3.

title: Auto Scaling Group
content:
Auto Scaling Group The Auto Scaling Group uses the Launch Template to create EC2 application instances in private application subnets and attaches those instances to the Target Group created earlier.
Item Configuration Launch Template sportbooking-lt, version Default (1). Subnets Two private application subnets in two AZs. Desired capacity 2. Min/Max Min 2, Max 4. Target Group sportbooking-tg. Health check EC2 and Elastic Load Balancing; 300-second grace period. Scaling policy Target tracking on Average CPU utilization, target 60, 300-second warmup. Set both desired and minimum capacity to 2 so the ASG maintains two instances across two Availability Zones. Maximum capacity 4 leaves room for the target tracking policy to scale out when average CPU exceeds the target.
Auto Scaling Group Procedure Use the completed Launch Template to deploy instances in private application subnets and register them automatically with the Target Group for health checking.
Procedure:
Start creating the Auto Scaling Group
Open EC2 > Auto Scaling Groups, then choose Create Auto Scaling group.
Figure 1: Start creating the Auto Scaling Group
The ASG maintains EC2 application capacity, replaces unhealthy instances, and supports rollouts through Instance Refresh. Select the Launch Template
Enter sportbooking-asg as the ASG name, select sportbooking-lt as the Launch Template, and select version Default (1).
Figure 2: Select the Launch Template
The ASG creates instances from the Launch Template. An incorrect version can cause instances to use outdated User Data or an outdated AMI. Confirm the template version
Review the AMI, instance type, key pair, and Security Group summary, then choose Next.
Figure 3: Confirm the template version
This is the final template check before the ASG creates instances in private subnets. Select the VPC and private application subnets
Select the SportBooking VPC and private application subnets in multiple AZs.
Figure 4: Select the VPC and private application subnets
EC2 application instances do not need public IP addresses. Private subnet placement ensures that only the ALB can call the application. Configure advanced capacity options
Select Balanced best effort as the distribution strategy, retain Default for Capacity Reservation preference, and choose Next.
Figure 5: Configure advanced capacity options
This deployment prioritizes a simple and stable configuration. A production environment can use mixed instances or Spot Instances to optimize cost. Attach load balancing
Enable load balancer integration, then attach the ASG to the existing sportbooking-tg Target Group.
Figure 6: Attach load balancing
The ASG must register EC2 instances with the Target Group so the ALB can forward traffic and perform health checks. Configure health checks
Enable Elastic Load Balancing health checks, set the health check grace period to 300 seconds, and choose Next.
Figure 7: Configure health checks
A grace period that is too short can cause the ASG to replace an instance before the application finishes starting. Configure group size and scaling limits
Set desired capacity to 2, minimum capacity to 2, and maximum capacity to 4.
Figure 8: Configure group size and scaling
Maintain two baseline instances while allowing the ASG to scale out to four instances. Configure the target tracking policy
Select Target tracking scaling policy, select Average CPU utilization, set the target value to 60, and set instance warmup to 300 seconds.
Figure 9: Configure target tracking for 60% average CPU
This policy adjusts desired capacity between two and four instances based on average CPU utilization across the Auto Scaling Group. Complete the Auto Scaling Group options
Keep deletion protection at None (default) and do not use a placement group. Review monitoring, warmup, and instance-maintenance options, then choose Next.
Figure 10: Review maintenance options and continue
Complete these options before configuring notifications and tags. Skip notifications during ASG creation No notification is added during ASG creation. Choose Next.
Figure 11: Skip or add notifications
The SNS channel is created separately in section 5.7 and used as an action for the CloudWatch alarm. Add an ASG tag Add a tag such as Project=SportBooking, then choose Next.
Figure 12: Add a tag to the ASG
Tags support resource filtering, cost management, and identification of project instances. Review and create the ASG Review the Launch Template, subnets, Target Group, capacity, and tags, then choose Create Auto Scaling group.
Figure 13: Review and create the ASG
Verify the Launch Template, private subnets, Target Group, capacity, and tags before creating the ASG. Verify the ASG after creation Open the new ASG and inspect its instances, desired capacity, Launch Template, and health status.
Figure 14: Verify the ASG after creation
This provides evidence that the compute layer is running and ready to receive traffic through the Target Group and ALB. Instance Refresh Procedure When updating the application version, build the JAR again and overwrite the release object in S3:
.\mvnw.cmd clean package -DskipTests aws s3 cp target\SportBooingSystem-0.0.1-SNAPSHOT.jar ` s3://sportbooking-artifacts-3stars-us-east-1/releases/SportBooingSystem-0.0.1-SNAPSHOT.jar ` --region us-east-1 Then open EC2 > Auto Scaling Groups > sportbooking-asg > Instance refresh > Start and use:
Use current Auto Scaling group configuration. Instance warmup: 300 seconds. Skip matching: Off. Monitor the Target Group until the replacement instances become Healthy. When an empty Launch Template version caused an error, update the ASG to a valid version before restarting the refresh with the current ASG configuration.

title: CloudFront Evaluation
content:
CloudFront Evaluation Result An attempt was made to configure CloudFront with the ALB as an origin to evaluate placing a CDN in front of the website. The AWS Console required account verification before a new CloudFront resource could be created. As a result, no distribution was created and CloudFront was not part of the tested request path.
The sportbooking-alb-2038054256.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com DNS name shown in the CloudFront image belongs to an earlier test ALB configuration. The completed configuration uses sportbooking-alb-1198360694.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com, and Route 53 points directly to this ALB.
Procedure:
Record the account-verification error during CloudFront creation
Open Create distribution and select the ALB as the test origin. Review the cache settings and security protections, then record the message requiring account verification before CloudFront resources can be added.
Figure 1: CloudFront was not created because account verification was incomplete
The evaluation stops at this screen. CloudFront and WAF remain target-architecture extensions and are not reported as deployed services.

title: Data and Access
content:
Deployment Scope Provision the foundational services before deploying EC2: create the S3 buckets and upload the artifact, create the S3 Gateway Endpoint, deploy Amazon RDS for MySQL Multi-AZ, create a Secrets Manager secret for application configuration, and then grant the EC2 IAM Role access to the resources that now exist.
Implementation Order Create S3 Buckets, Upload the Artifact, and Configure the Gateway Endpoint Create Private Amazon RDS for MySQL Create the Application Secret in Secrets Manager Create the EC2 IAM Role Create the secret after RDS so DB_URL contains the correct database endpoint. Create the IAM Role after S3 and the secret so its policy can reference ARNs that already exist. Create the Launch Template and EC2 resources only after the artifact, RDS, secret, and role are all ready.
Recorded Results The S3 artifact bucket contains the JAR under the releases/ prefix and is not public. The S3 upload bucket stores football-pitch images and has Block Public Access enabled. The S3 Gateway Endpoint is associated with the route tables of the private application subnets. The sportbooking-ec2-role IAM Role permits SSM access, S3 artifact reads, S3 upload reads and writes, and secret reads. Amazon RDS for MySQL is in private database subnets and is not publicly accessible. The sportbooking/prod/app-env secret contains the configuration required for EC2 to create /opt/sportbooking/app.env.

title: IAM Role
content:
Deployment Scope Create an IAM Role that allows the EC2 application to read the artifact from S3, read and write uploaded files, retrieve configuration from Secrets Manager, and connect through Systems Manager Session Manager. This removes the need to store access keys directly on EC2.
Role Configuration Item Configuration value Trusted entity AWS service: EC2 Role name sportbooking-ec2-role Managed policy AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore Inline policy Read the S3 artifact, read/write S3 uploads, and read the secret Attached to EC2 Through the IAM instance profile in the Launch Template Application Inline Policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "ReadArtifactJarFromS3", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": ["s3:GetObject"], "Resource": ["arn:aws:s3:::sportbooking-artifacts-3stars-us-east-1/releases/*"] }, { "Sid": "AccessUploadFilesInS3", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject", "s3:DeleteObject"], "Resource": ["arn:aws:s3:::sportbooking-uploads-3stars-us-east-1/uploads/*"] }, { "Sid": "ReadAppEnvSecret", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": ["secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"], "Resource": ["arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:<account-id>:secret:sportbooking/prod/app-env*"] } ] } Create the IAM Role after the S3 buckets and secret. This allows the custom policy to be checked directly against the artifact bucket, upload bucket, and sportbooking/prod/app-env ARNs before it is attached to the role.
Procedure Start with an EC2 trust relationship, attach the SSM policy, and then attach a policy that limits access to the required S3 and Secrets Manager resources.
Procedure:
Start creating the IAM Role
Open IAM > Roles, then choose Create role.
Figure 1: Start creating the IAM Role
EC2 requires permission to read S3, read Secrets Manager, and use Session Manager without storing access keys on the instance. Select EC2 as the trusted entity
Select AWS service, choose the EC2 use case, and choose Next.
Figure 2: Select EC2 as the trusted entity
The trusted entity determines which service can assume the role. Selecting EC2 allows instances to receive temporary credentials through the instance profile. Find the required managed policy
Search for the SSM policy under Add permissions, then select AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore.
Figure 3: Find the required managed policy
The SSM policy supports private EC2 administration through Session Manager without opening public SSH. Create the application permissions policy
Open the policy creation workflow or add an inline policy. Enter the JSON permissions for reading the S3 artifact, reading and writing S3 uploads, and reading the secret.
Figure 4: Create the application permissions policy
Grant access only to the SportBooking buckets, prefixes, and secret; do not grant `s3:*` or `secretsmanager:*` across the entire account. Name and create the policy
Enter sportbooking-ec2-app-policy as the policy name, review the permissions, and choose Create policy.
Figure 5: Name and create the policy
A descriptive name makes the policy's purpose clear during an audit. Return to permission selection
Refresh the policy list after creating the new policy, then search for sportbooking-ec2-app-policy.
Figure 6: Return to permission selection
The new policy appears after the list refresh and can be selected for the role. Attach the policies to the role
Select the custom policy, confirm that both the SSM and application policies are selected, and choose Next.
Figure 7: Attach the policies to the role
The role requires both SSM operational permissions and application runtime permissions. Name the role
Enter sportbooking-ec2-role as the role name and add a description of its purpose.
Figure 8: Name the role
This role is selected as the IAM instance profile in the Launch Template and remains separate from roles used by other services. Review and create the role
Verify that the trust policy specifies EC2, review the permission policies, and choose Create role.
Figure 9: Review and create the role
The final review confirms that EC2 is the trusted entity and that the role has both operational and application permissions. Confirm that the role is ready
Find sportbooking-ec2-role in the role list, open it, and verify its permissions.
Figure 10: Confirm that the role is ready
The role is attached to the Launch Template so every EC2 instance in the Auto Scaling Group receives the required permissions at startup.

title: NAT and Endpoint Cleanup
content:
13. Delete the NAT Gateways Both NAT Gateways were deleted before the Elastic IPs and VPC. Each NAT Gateway had to reach Deleted before its Elastic IP was no longer associated.
Procedure:
Select the NAT Gateway in the first Availability Zone
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) select sportbooking-nat-a; (2) open Actions; and (3) select Delete NAT gateway.
Figure 1: Select the first NAT Gateway to delete
The private EC2 instances have terminated, so Internet access through the NAT Gateway is no longer required. Confirm deletion of the first NAT Gateway
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) enter delete; and (2) select Delete.
Figure 2: Confirm deletion of the first NAT Gateway
The NAT Gateway changes to `Deleting` while AWS processes the request. Select the NAT Gateway in the second Availability Zone
Check the deletion notification for the first NAT Gateway. Then (1) select sportbooking-nat-b; (2) open Actions; and (3) select Delete NAT gateway.
Figure 3: Select the second NAT Gateway to delete
The architecture uses one NAT Gateway in each of two Availability Zones, so both resources must be removed. Confirm deletion of the second NAT Gateway
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) enter delete; and (2) select Delete.
Figure 4: Confirm deletion of the second NAT Gateway
Deleting both NAT Gateways removes the network resources that otherwise continue to incur hourly charges. Verify the status of both NAT Gateways
Refresh the list until both sportbooking-nat-a and sportbooking-nat-b display Deleted.
Figure 5: Verify that both NAT Gateways have been deleted
Release the Elastic IPs only after NAT Gateway deletion is complete. If attempted too early, the addresses remain associated and cannot be released. Refresh the status periodically and release the Elastic IPs only after both NAT Gateways display Deleted.
14. Release the Elastic IPs Procedure:
Select the Elastic IPs used by the NAT Gateways
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) select sportbooking-eip-us-east-1a and sportbooking-eip-us-east-1b; (2) open Actions; and (3) select Release Elastic IP addresses.
Figure 6: Select the Elastic IPs to release
After the NAT Gateways are deleted, the two Elastic IPs are no longer associated and should be returned to AWS. Confirm release of the Elastic IPs
Verify both IP addresses and Allocation IDs, then select Release.
Figure 7: Confirm release of the Elastic IPs
A released Elastic IP no longer belongs to the AWS account and can be allocated to another account. Verify the Elastic IP list
Confirm the Elastic IP addresses released message, then verify that no project Elastic IP remains in the Region.
Figure 8: Verify that the Elastic IPs have been released
This result confirms that the NAT layer no longer has dedicated public IPv4 addresses allocated. 15. Delete the S3 Gateway VPC Endpoint Procedure:
Select the VPC Endpoint
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) select sportbooking-vpce-s3; (2) open Actions; and (3) select Delete VPC endpoints.
Figure 9: Select the S3 Gateway VPC Endpoint to delete
The EC2 instances and S3 artifact bucket have been processed, so the endpoint has no remaining traffic to serve. Deleting the endpoint also removes its entries from the associated route tables. Confirm endpoint deletion
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) enter delete; and (2) select Delete.
Figure 10: Confirm S3 Gateway VPC Endpoint deletion
The endpoint is permanently deleted and cannot be restored from its current configuration. Verify that the endpoint has been deleted
Confirm the Successfully deleted endpoints message, then verify that sportbooking-vpce-s3 no longer appears in the list.
Figure 11: Verify that the S3 Gateway VPC Endpoint has been deleted
The VPC no longer contains a custom endpoint that could prevent the final deletion.

title: Troubleshooting
content: Recorded Errors and Resolutions Error Identified cause Resolution JAR upload returns NoSuchBucket The S3 bucket does not exist or the bucket name is incorrect. Create the artifact bucket first and verify the S3 URI. Invalid bucket name <artifact-bucket> The release command still contains a placeholder. Replace the placeholder with sportbooking-artifacts-3stars-us-east-1. RDS missing table The database schema does not contain a table required by Hibernate. Check ddl-auto and application logs, then create the required seed data. S3 Region displays literal ${AWS_REGION} The Region variable was not passed to the application. Add AWS_REGION, AWS_DEFAULT_REGION, and AWS_S3_REGION=us-east-1. /uploads images return 404 The S3 controller is disabled, the bucket or prefix is incorrect, or IAM permissions are missing. Verify APP_STORAGE_TYPE=s3, the bucket, uploads prefix, and IAM policy. Registration fails on membership levels The membership_levels table has no data. Insert the NONE, SILVER, GOLD, and DIAMOND levels. ACM remains Pending validation The DNS validation CNAME is not public or the domain is misspelled. Verify the hosted zone, registrar name servers, and validation CNAME. Instance Refresh version is empty Desired configuration has no Launch Template version. Update the ASG to a valid version and use the current ASG configuration. Well-Architected Framework Review Pillar Application in the architecture Security Private subnets for EC2 and RDS; least-privilege Security Groups; Secrets Manager; IAM Role instead of access keys; Session Manager without SSH; HTTPS through ACM. Reliability ALB, ASG, and two AZs; Target Group health checks; RDS backups and snapshots; Instance Refresh replaces instances. Operational Excellence User Data automates bootstrap; S3 stores deployment artifacts; curl and systemctl provide verification; CloudWatch and SNS support operations. Cost Optimization ASG desired capacity is reduced to 0 before deletion; RDS and NAT Gateways are removed after testing; resource sizes are recorded for cost control. Performance Efficiency The ALB distributes requests; S3 separates uploads from EC2; CloudFront remains a target-architecture extension because no distribution was created. Operational Verification Commands ipconfig /flushdns nslookup -type=NS younglilliu.id.vn nslookup sport.younglilliu.id.vn curl.exe -I http://sport.younglilliu.id.vn curl.exe -I https://sport.younglilliu.id.vn sudo -i systemctl status sportbooking --no-pager journalctl -u sportbooking -n 100 --no-pager curl -I http://127.0.0.1:8080/ curl http://127.0.0.1:8080/actuator/health

title: EC2 and Load Balancing
content:
Deployment Scope Deploy the compute layer only after S3 contains the artifact and the IAM Role, RDS, and Secrets Manager resources are ready. The sequence is: create a seed EC2 instance to verify the runtime and permissions; create an AMI and Launch Template; create the Target Group and Application Load Balancer; and finally create the Auto Scaling Group in private application subnets.
Create the Auto Scaling Group after the Target Group and ALB because the ASG is associated with the Target Group during configuration. This order automatically registers and health-checks new EC2 instances as they launch.
Contents Seed EC2 Instance Launch Template Target Group and ALB Auto Scaling Group The pages follow resource dependencies. The seed EC2 instance validates the runtime and access permissions before the AMI is created; the Launch Template is completed before the Auto Scaling Group; and the Target Group and ALB must be ready before the ASG registers instances.

title: Security Groups and VPC
content:
16. Remove Referencing Rules and Delete the Security Groups A Security Group cannot be deleted while it is attached to an ENI or referenced by another Security Group. The ALB, EC2 instances, and RDS database were deleted before this section was performed.
Procedure:
Open the outbound rules of the ALB Security Group
Open the sportbooking-alb Security Group. Select the Outbound rules tab, then select Edit outbound rules.
Figure 1: Open the outbound rules of the ALB Security Group
The outbound rule on port `8080` references the EC2 App Security Group. This reference must be removed before the project Security Groups can be deleted together. Delete the referencing outbound rule
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) select Delete for the port 8080 rule that targets the App Security Group; and (2) select Save rules.
Figure 2: Delete the outbound rule that references the App Security Group
Removing the rule breaks the reference from the ALB Security Group to the App Security Group. Open the inbound rules of the ALB Security Group
Select the Inbound rules tab, then select Edit inbound rules.
Figure 3: Open the inbound rules of the ALB Security Group
The two public HTTP and HTTPS rules were removed to close all inbound access to the Security Group before deletion. Delete the remaining inbound rules
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) delete the HTTPS rule on port 443; (2) delete the HTTP rule on port 80; and (3) select Save rules.
Figure 4: Delete the inbound rules of the ALB Security Group
CIDR-based rules do not create dependencies between Security Groups, but deleting them confirms that the Security Group permits no traffic while cleanup is in progress. Select the project Security Groups
Review and remove any remaining references among sportbooking-app, sportbooking-rds, and sportbooking-alb in the same manner. Then (1) select the three project Security Groups; and (2) open Actions and select Delete security groups.
Figure 5: Select the SportBooking Security Groups
Do not select the `default` Security Group or a Security Group from another VPC. If AWS reports a dependency, check the remaining ENIs and referencing rules before retrying. Confirm Security Group deletion
Verify that the list contains sportbooking-rds, sportbooking-app, and sportbooking-alb. Then (1) enter delete; and (2) select Delete.
Figure 6: Confirm deletion of the Security Groups
Deleting the Security Groups together after removing references prevents unused network configurations from remaining. Verify the result
Confirm the Successfully deleted 3 security groups message, then verify that only default Security Groups or Security Groups from other environments remain.
Figure 7: Verify that the Security Groups have been deleted
This result confirms that the ENIs and Security Group associations of the ALB, EC2 instances, and RDS database have been released. 17. Delete the DB Subnet Group The DB Subnet Group was deleted after RDS because a DB instance using the subnet group prevents its deletion.
Procedure:
Select the DB Subnet Group
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) open RDS > Subnet groups and select sportbooking-db-subnet-group; and (2) select Delete.
Figure 8: Select the DB Subnet Group to delete
RDS deletion has completed, so no database continues to reference the subnet group. Confirm DB Subnet Group deletion
Verify the name sportbooking-db-subnet-group, then select Delete.
Figure 9: Confirm DB Subnet Group deletion
This action deletes only the RDS DB Subnet Group configuration; it does not directly delete the VPC subnets. Verify that the DB Subnet Group has been deleted
Confirm the successful deletion message, then verify that only default subnet groups or subnet groups from other environments remain.
Figure 10: Verify that the DB Subnet Group has been deleted
The VPC no longer contains a custom RDS configuration that depends on the private subnets. 18. Delete the VPC The VPC was deleted last, after the compute, database, endpoint, NAT Gateway, and Security Group resources had been processed.
Procedure:
Select the project VPC
On the console, perform the following in order: (1) open VPC > Your VPCs and select sportbooking-vpc; (2) open Actions; and (3) select Delete VPC.
Figure 11: Select the SportBooking VPC to delete
The name, VPC ID, and CIDR were cross-checked to avoid selecting the default VPC or a VPC from another environment. Review dependent resources and confirm
Review the resources that will be deleted with the VPC, including the remaining Internet Gateway, subnets, and route tables. Then (1) enter delete; and (2) select Delete.
Figure 12: Review dependent resources and confirm VPC deletion
The dialog shows `sportbooking-vpc` and 12 related network resources in the deletion request. Every listed resource was verified as belonging to the project. Verify that the VPC has been deleted
Confirm the successfully deleted sportbooking-vpc and 12 other resources message, then verify that only the default VPC or other VPCs that must be retained remain.
Figure 13: Verify that the SportBooking VPC has been deleted
This step completes the cleanup of the SportBooking network infrastructure. If the VPC cannot be deleted, check for remaining ENIs under EC2 > Network Interfaces, VPC Endpoints, NAT Gateways, Load Balancers, RDS resources, cross-referencing Security Groups, and resources managed by other services. Do not delete the default VPC to resolve a dependency error.

title: Workshop
content:
Deploying the SportBooking Football Pitch Booking Website on AWS Website Feature Demo Video Report Scope This report presents the deployment of SportBookingSystem on AWS using a basic production architecture. The website is published through an HTTPS domain; traffic passes through Route 53 and an Application Load Balancer; the Spring Boot application runs on EC2 instances in private subnets; business data is stored in Amazon RDS for MySQL; release artifacts and uploaded images are stored in Amazon S3; and sensitive configuration is managed in AWS Secrets Manager.
The content follows the order in which resources were provisioned: prepare the source code; build the network and security layers; create S3 buckets and upload the artifact; create RDS; store configuration in Secrets Manager; grant IAM permissions; deploy EC2, the ALB, and the Auto Scaling Group; configure DNS and HTTPS; establish monitoring; and finally remove the resources.
The evidence images do not expose database passwords, OAuth client secrets, tokens, access keys, or secret values. The report retains only resource names, Region, status, ports, Security Group sources, and non-sensitive configuration.
Deployment Architecture Component Role Route 53 Manages DNS for sport.younglilliu.id.vn and points an Alias record to the ALB. AWS Certificate Manager Issues the TLS certificate used to enable HTTPS on the ALB. Application Load Balancer Accepts public HTTP/HTTPS traffic, redirects HTTP to HTTPS, and forwards requests to the Target Group. Auto Scaling Group + EC2 Runs the Spring Boot JAR in private application subnets and replaces instances during rollouts. Amazon RDS for MySQL Stores application data in private database subnets. Amazon S3 Stores the release JAR and images uploaded through the website. Secrets Manager Stores the database URL, database credentials, domain, S3 configuration, and OAuth configuration. IAM Role + SSM Allows EC2 to read S3 and Secrets Manager and be administered through Session Manager without opening SSH. CloudWatch + SNS Monitors operational status and sends notifications when metrics or alarms cross configured thresholds. CloudFront and AWS WAF Components of the target architecture that were evaluated but were not included in the primary request path for this deployment. Contents Architecture Preparation Network and Security Data and Access EC2 and Load Balancing Domain and HTTPS Operations Cleanup

content:

title: Feedback and Contributions
content:
General Evaluation 1. Working Environment The working environment is very friendly and open. Members of FCAJ are always ready to support me when I face difficulties, even outside of working hours. The workspace is neat and comfortable, which helps me focus better.
2. Support from Mentors / Team Admins The organizing committee always provides the best conditions regarding documentation and practical resources (AWS lab accounts) for interns to operate smoothly. Especially, I highly appreciate the guiding methods of the Mentors. Instead of just giving the solutions, they always ask counter-questions and allow interns the freedom to experiment, make mistakes, and self-debug on the system to find the root cause of the problem.
3. Alignment between Work and Major The tasks in the Bootcamp perfectly complement the Information Technology foundation I learned at school. Not only does it consolidate logical thinking and system architecture, but the program also opens up entirely new aspects that are rarely practiced deeply at school, such as cloud computing. Thanks to this, I can both reinforce my foundational knowledge and learn practical skills.
4. Opportunities for Learning & Skill Development This internship is a huge leap in both hard and soft skills. Besides mastering AWS services, I also learned how to work effectively in a team, use progress management tools, and especially professional presentation and communication skills that meet the standards of a multinational tech corporation. Mentors also shared many practical experiences to help me better orient my career.
5. Culture & Team Spirit The company culture is very positive: everyone respects each other, working seriously but still having fun.
6. Policies / Benefits for Interns The company provides an internship allowance and flexible working hours when necessary. Additionally, being able to participate in meetups is a huge plus.
Other Questions What are you most satisfied with during your internship? It is the opportunity to directly “touch” the core technologies leading the trends (Cloud, AI) and to interact and learn professional working mindsets from top engineers and solution experts in the industry.
What do you think the company needs to improve for future interns? For some labs with complex architectures, the tutorial videos are not really effective. If introducing it to friends, would you recommend they intern here? Why? Definitely YES. This is a perfect launchpad and a quality “guarantee” for any IT student who wants to step out of their comfort zone, grasp cloud technology trends, and map out a clear advancement path for their career.
Proposals & Expectations Proposals: I hope that in upcoming Bootcamps, the program can organize more Q&A sessions to resolve difficulties during self-study or provide direct orientation to help interns feel more confident during their internship. Future Orientation: The precious experiences at FCAJ are a solid preparation for my upcoming graduation internship. I strongly desire to continue honing my skills and have the opportunity to accompany the AWS ecosystem in my software development career (Backend/Cloud Engineer) in the future.

title: Cleanup
content:
Cleanup Scope After deployment, testing, and evidence collection were completed, the SportBooking resources were decommissioned in dependency order. The artifact bucket and the compute and network layers were removed. The upload bucket, final RDS snapshot, and retained automated backup were intentionally preserved to protect data required for recovery.
Deleting the Auto Scaling Group, RDS database, S3 bucket, VPC, and IAM role can cause data loss or make the website completely unavailable. Perform these actions only after the required data has been backed up, the report has been completed, and the environment is confirmed to be no longer in use.
Cleanup Order Phase Resources Dependency 1 Route 53, CloudWatch, and SNS Stop directing users to the application; delete the alarm before the SNS topic. 2 ASG, ALB, Target Group, and Launch Template Delete the ASG before the Launch Template; delete the ALB before the Target Group. 3 IAM, RDS, S3, Secrets Manager, and ACM EC2 has stopped; ACM is no longer used by the ALB; no CloudFront distribution was created. 4 NAT Gateway, Elastic IP, and VPC Endpoint Wait until each NAT Gateway reaches Deleted before releasing its Elastic IP. 5 Security Group, DB Subnet Group, and VPC The ALB, EC2 instances, RDS database, endpoint, and Security Group references have been removed. 6 Remaining-resource review No snapshot, backup, log group, hosted zone, or policy remains outside the retention plan. Verify the correct AWS account and Region us-east-1 before every action. Select only resources named for the SportBooking project; do not delete default or shared resources.
Preparation Before Deletion Identify the final RDS snapshot, retained automated backup, and data in the upload bucket that must be preserved. Stop the Alias record sport.younglilliu.id.vn from routing to the ALB before deleting the load-balancing layer. Confirm that no CloudFront distribution was created, so no CloudFront dependency needs to be removed. Retain the public hosted zone because the domain remains managed; remove only records that are no longer used. Record every retained storage resource so that it is not mistaken for an overlooked resource. Contents Monitoring Application and Load Balancing Data and Configuration NAT Gateways and VPC Endpoint Security Groups and VPC Final Verification
